name: Dr Kayeleigh Sharp
focus: Andean archaeology • Economic complementarity • Underrepresented societies
bio: Dr. Kayeleigh Sharp is an anthropological archaeologist with a long-standing interest in Latin America and ancient Andean civilization. In 2010 she began what has become a long-term research project in the Lambayeque region on the north coast of Peru, one of the two centers of primary cultural development in the Central Andes. Her fieldwork and research focus on academically overlooked, or 'underrepresented,' archaeological groups—those who lived during the first millennium, in particular. The primary aim of her work is to expose the underlying social and economic relationships that characterize the region's dynamic past through the lens of those who lived and worked together and interacted across social boundaries. These complexities underlie the phenomenon she calls 'economic complementarity,' a term that refers to the mutually interdependent production, distribution, and consumption of goods, and the complementary social relationships that support them.
